understand.plain: The function takes two integers x and y passed by value. It sets x to 0 locally, makes a pointer point to x, copies the value of x (which is 0) into y, then sets x to 1 via the pointer. It then prints x and y. Because x and y are local copies, changes don't affect the caller.

keywords.explain: The function receives copies of the arguments; modifying them does not change the caller's variables.
keywords.example: printxy(1, 1) — the 1s are copied into local x and y inside the function.

keywords.explain: *ptr reads or writes the value at the memory address stored in ptr.
keywords.example: If ptr = &x and x = 0, then *ptr is 0; assigning *ptr = 1 sets x to 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='38' class='lbl ink'>Step-by-step execution of printxy(1,1)</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='120' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='74' class='sub ink'>x (local) = 1</text><rect x='160' y='50' width='120' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='74' class='sub ink'>y (local) = 1</text><text x='20' y='112' class='sub ink'>x = 0  →  x becomes 0</text><text x='20' y='132' class='sub ink'>ptr = &x  →  ptr points to x</text><text x='20' y='152' class='sub ink'>y = *ptr  →  y = 0   (x is currently 0)</text><text x='20' y='172' class='sub ink'>*ptr = 1  →  x = 1   (via ptr)</text><text x='20' y='196' class='tag ink'>printf: x=1, y=0  →  Output: 1, 0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Execution trace showing local x going 1->0->1 via pointer, and y getting assigned 0 from *ptr before x becomes 1.

given.aim: Determine the values of x and y printed inside printxy after all pointer operations.

terms.meaning: Local integer variable, initially 1 (argument), then reassigned to 0, then to 1 via pointer.
terms.example: x=0 after first assignment; x=1 after *ptr=1.
terms.connects: x is the first value printed.

terms.meaning: Local integer variable, initially 1 (argument), then reassigned to *ptr which is 0 at that moment.
terms.example: y = *ptr = 0 because x is 0 when this line executes.
terms.connects: y is the second value printed.

terms.meaning: Pointer to int, set to address of local x.
terms.example: ptr = &x; so *ptr and x refer to the same memory.

given.plan: Trace each statement in order, keeping track of the current value of x and y. Note that y is assigned *ptr BEFORE *ptr is changed to 1.

steps.title: Trace initial setup
steps.apply: Function called with x=1, y=1 (copies). x=0 sets local x to 0. ptr=&x makes ptr point to x.
steps.note: At this point x=0, y=1, ptr points to x.

steps.title: Assign y = *ptr
steps.note: This captures x's value BEFORE x is changed to 1.

steps.title: Assign *ptr = 1
steps.apply: Setting *ptr = 1 sets x to 1 (since ptr = &x). Now x=1, y=0.
steps.note: y was already assigned 0 in the previous step, so it is unaffected.

questions.formula_note: Pointer arithmetic and pass-by-value semantics in C: local variable modified through a pointer, while the other local variable captured the old value.

questions.to_find: The value of array[0] printed after the sorting loop completes

understand.plain: The program runs a modified bubble sort that swaps adjacent elements when the left element is SMALLER than the right — this sorts in descending order. The loop continues until no swaps are made in either pass. Finally it prints array[0], which will be the largest element.

keywords.explain: Swapping when array[i] < array[i+1] moves larger values to the front, resulting in descending order.
A flag set to 1 before each outer loop pass; reset to 0 if any swap occurs. Loop ends when no swaps happen.
keywords.example: If a full forward and backward pass completes without any swap, done stays 1 and the while loop exits.

understand.visual_alt: Bidirectional bubble sort on {3,5,1,4,6,2} with descending condition; after sorting, array is {6,5,4,3,2,1} so array[0]=6.

given.aim: Find array[0] after sorting {3,5,1,4,6,2} with this modified bidirectional bubble sort.

terms.connects: This is the data being sorted.

terms.meaning: Swap when the current element is less than the next, pushing larger values leftward.
terms.connects: This condition causes the array to sort in descending order.

terms.meaning: After all passes complete with no swaps, the array is fully sorted descending: {6,5,4,3,2,1}.
terms.connects: The program prints array[0], which is 6.

given.plan: Recognize that swapping when left < right sorts descending. The maximum of the array is 6. After sorting, array[0] = 6.

steps.title: Identify sort direction
steps.apply: Swapping when left < right moves larger elements toward index 0. This is descending-order bubble sort.
steps.note: Contrast with ascending sort which swaps when left > right.

steps.title: Trace Pass 1 (backward)
steps.note: Multiple passes are needed; the algorithm is a cocktail shaker sort variant sorting descending.

steps.title: Determine final answer
steps.apply: The algorithm is a descending bidirectional bubble sort. It terminates only when fully sorted: {6,5,4,3,2,1}. Therefore array[0] = 6.
steps.note: The maximum element (6) ends up at index 0 in a descending sort.

questions.formula_note: Bidirectional bubble sort (cocktail shaker sort) with descending swap condition; result is the maximum element at index 0.

questions.to_find: The integer value printed by printf in main

understand.plain: The function jumble(x, y) returns 2*x + y. The program calls jumble twice: first with (y, x) = (5, 2) storing the result back in y, then with (new_y, x) = (12, 2) storing the result in x. It then prints x.

keywords.explain: jumble receives copies of y and x; changes inside jumble do not affect main's variables directly.
keywords.example: jumble(5, 2) computes 2*5+2=12 and returns 12; main's y is updated to 12 by assignment.

keywords.explain: The computed 2*x+y is returned and assigned in the caller.
keywords.example: y = jumble(y, x) means y gets the return value of jumble.

understand.visual_alt: Call 1: jumble(5,2)=12, y=12. Call 2: jumble(12,2)=26, x=26. Print 26.

given.aim: Evaluate the two calls to jumble and determine the final value of x printed.

terms.meaning: The function doubles the first argument and adds the second.
terms.connects: Applied twice with updated arguments to get final x.

terms.meaning: Starting state of main's local variables.
terms.example: x=2, y=5 before any jumble call.
terms.connects: These are the arguments for the first call.

terms.example: First arg is main's y=5, second is main's x=2.
terms.connects: This updated y is used as the first argument in the second call.

given.plan: Evaluate jumble(y, x) = jumble(5, 2) = 2*5+2 = 12, set y=12. Then evaluate jumble(y, x) = jumble(12, 2) = 2*12+2 = 26, set x=26. Print x=26.

steps.title: First call: y = jumble(y, x) = jumble(5, 2)
steps.apply: jumble(5, 2): x_local = 2*5 + 2 = 12. Returns 12. Assign: y = 12. (main's x still = 2)
steps.note: Note: jumble's first argument corresponds to jumble's parameter x; here it receives main's y=5.

steps.title: Second call: x = jumble(y, x) = jumble(12, 2)

questions.formula_note: Function call evaluation and pass-by-value semantics: each call computes 2*first_arg + second_arg and the result is assigned back in main.
